skill.name: visual-qa
skill.description: Automates responsive visual QA by capturing animated page screenshots across desktop, tablet, and mobile viewports.
skill.summary: Capture responsive screenshots after scrolling pages to trigger animations for visual review.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-severity patterns, but the weak-crypto and system-reconnaissance hits are false positives in prose or viewport definitions. The confirmed risks are expected for a visual QA skill: it opens web pages, ignores HTTPS errors in browser contexts, documents shell usage, and writes screenshot files to a chosen output directory.

medium_findings.title: Browser Network Access for Visual QA
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The script navigates to configured and user-provided URLs to capture screenshots. This is required behavior, but it can contact remote hosts and load third-party resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Playwright navigation call and URL arguments clearly confirm network access. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Screenshot Files Written to User-Selected Directories
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The script creates output directories and writes PNG screenshots. This is expected, but a broad output path could create files in sensitive or cluttered locations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The output directory is a command-line argument and is passed into directory creation and screenshot writing. No deletion or secret-reading behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Browser Context Ignores HTTPS Certificate Errors
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Browser contexts are created with HTTPS errors ignored. This helps test local sites, but it can hide certificate problems during QA.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The option is explicit in both browser contexts. It is common for local testing, but it reduces security visibility when testing production URLs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged lines are viewport definitions, argparse text, and skill prose. No MD5, SHA1, encryption, or password hashing code was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines contain display sizes or descriptions, not cryptographic functions. The static label does not match the file content.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Command Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged shell execution lines are documentation examples showing how a user may run the screenshot script. SKILL.md does not execute these commands by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear inside Markdown code fences and plain text. They are still relevant operational guidance, but not automatic code execution.

low_findings.title: Absolute Local Paths in Documentation
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The documentation references root and home directory paths. This is not hidden-file access by the skill, but it is a portability and environment assumption issue.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are visible documentation references, not reads from hidden files. They may still guide users toward privileged or non-portable locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged line is a visual QA checklist item about status badges on a portfolio page. No host, process, network, or system discovery command appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is ordinary checklist prose. There is no semantic evidence of reconnaissance behavior at this location.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled URL Navigation
dangerous_patterns.description: The --url and --base-url arguments allow browser navigation to caller-selected pages. Users should only test trusted sites or approved environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The navigation sink and command-line URL sources are both visible. The risk depends on user choice of target site.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The --output argument controls where screenshots are written. Users should choose a dedicated project directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The output argument flows into screenshot file creation. Filenames are generated by the script, which limits but does not remove path risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTPS Errors Ignored During Browser Testing
dangerous_patterns.description: The Playwright browser contexts ignore HTTPS errors. This can mask certificate failures during production visual QA.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The configuration is explicit and appears in both single-page and all-page flows. It is useful locally but weakens QA for certificate checks.

content.value_statement: Visual regressions are hard to catch after CSS, template, or animation changes. This skill captures responsive screenshots after scrolling pages so Claude, Codex, or Claude Code can review visible issues.
actual_capabilities: Captures screenshots for one URL or a fixed set of site pages.
Tests desktop, tablet, and mobile viewport sizes using Playwright Chromium.
Scrolls through pages before capture to trigger scroll-based animations.
Saves full-page PNG screenshots with timestamped filenames.
Provides a checklist for reviewing headers, menus, images, forms, and layout issues.
limitations: It is configured for specific site paths unless users provide another base URL.
It captures screenshots but does not compare images against baselines.
It requires Python, Playwright, and Chromium to be installed.
It ignores HTTPS certificate errors during browser capture.

use_cases.title: Review a marketing site update
use_cases.description: Capture desktop and mobile screenshots after changing CSS, templates, or page sections.

use_cases.title: Check responsive layout before release
use_cases.description: Generate screenshots across common device widths and review text, images, spacing, and menus.

use_cases.title: Document visual issues for stakeholders
use_cases.description: Create timestamped screenshots that support QA notes, bug reports, and client review.

prompt_templates.title: Capture one page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a single URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run visual QA for this page: [URL]. Save screenshots in a project screenshots folder and summarize visible layout issues.

prompt_templates.title: Check all configured pages
prompt_templates.prompt: Run visual QA on all configured pages for [BASE_URL]. Review desktop, tablet, and mobile screenshots for visual regressions.

prompt_templates.title: Focus on animations
prompt_templates.prompt: Capture screenshots after scrolling each page to trigger animations. Check for hidden text, unfinished transitions, and missing images.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare release QA notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Run full visual QA for [BASE_URL]. Group findings by page and viewport, then list blocking issues and minor polish items.

output_examples.input: Run visual QA on the About page after CSS changes.
output: Screenshots were captured for desktop, tablet, and mobile widths.
The desktop layout looks stable, but the mobile hero title wraps into the image area.
The team photo section loads correctly after scrolling.

output_examples.input: Check all pages on the staging site before release.
output: Home, About, Portfolio, Contact, and legal pages were reviewed across responsive viewports.
The Contact form is readable on mobile, but the Portfolio cards need more spacing on tablet.
No horizontal scrolling was observed in the captured screenshots.

output_examples.input: Review animation visibility on the landing page.
output: The page was scrolled before capture to trigger animation states.
Hero text and card content are visible in the final screenshots.
One lower section appears delayed and should be checked in the browser.

best_practices: Run the skill against trusted staging or local URLs before production release.
Use a dedicated screenshots folder so generated files are easy to review and delete.
Review screenshots by page and viewport to separate layout issues from content issues.
anti_patterns: Do not run it against private sites unless screenshots can be stored locally.
Do not use it as a replacement for accessibility, performance, or security testing.
Do not store generated screenshots in source control without review.

faq.question: What does this skill test?
faq.answer: It captures responsive screenshots and helps review layout, image, text, menu, form, and animation visibility issues.

faq.question: Does it require a browser?
faq.answer: Yes. The script uses Playwright with Chromium to open pages and capture full-page screenshots.

faq.question: Can I test a custom URL?
faq.answer: Yes. The script supports a single URL and a base URL for the configured page list.

faq.question: Does it compare screenshots automatically?
faq.answer: No. It captures screenshots for review but does not include baseline image comparison.

faq.question: Where are screenshots saved?
faq.answer: They are saved to the configured output directory with timestamped PNG filenames.

faq.question: Is it safe to run on production sites?
faq.answer: Use caution. It loads pages with a browser and stores screenshots, so staging or approved URLs are preferred.
